description: Make sure the voice finishes reading before actually going to the next item. Now it almost finishes, but on the last few syllables of the last word, or maybe the last syllable of the last word, it goes to the next one. So maybe add a one-second delay after finishing, and then go to the next item.

results.identified_issue: The feed auto-advanced the instant player.finishedItemID changed. That id is set from AVSpeechSynthesizer's didFinish (system TTS) and AVPlayerItemDidPlayToEndTime (rendered audio); the TTS callback in particular lands a hair before the final sample plays out, so advancing immediately scrolled to the next card and started its audio over the tail of the current one — exactly the last-syllable clipping reported. Fix delays only the advance (unlock stays immediate) via a cancellable Task, matching the lockMessageTask pattern already in the file. If this ever needs tuning, advanceDelayAfterFinish is a single named constant. Not built (vigil builds from the pushed commit); verified by reading. If a device still clips, the finish signal itself may be firing too early and completionSlop in ClipPreviewPlayer would be the next lever.
results.summary: Added a one-second pause after a card finishes before auto-advancing, so the last syllable isn't clipped
results.detailed_description: You were right that it was clipping the last syllable. The feed rolls hands-free onto the next item the moment the current card reports it finished playing — but for the system voice that 'finished' signal fires slightly before the very last bit of audio actually plays out, so the next card was scrolling in and starting its own audio right on top of the tail end of the last word. I added a one-second pause: when a card finishes, it now waits a beat and then advances, so the last word finishes cleanly first. The pause is cancellable, so if you scroll ahead yourself during that second, or the next card is already playing, it won't fight you or double-advance. The delay lives in one named constant (currently one second) so it's easy to nudge up or down. I couldn't run it on the device here (a separate watcher builds and deploys the pushed commit), so please give it a listen — if any word still gets cut off, tell me and I'll lengthen the pause or look at making the finish signal itself land later.
